papers.title: Improved catalytic efficiency of P450 OleP for converting lithocholic acid into murideoxycholic and ursodeoxycholic acids through semi-rational and rational design.
papers.abstract: Cytochrome P450 enzymes are promising for synthesizing therapeutically beneficial bile acids murideoxycholic acid (MDCA) and ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) from lithocholic acid (LCA). However, the catalytic efficiency of P450 enzymes remains inadequate for large-scale production. Here, we address this limitation by engineering P450 OleP, achieving high activity for the efficient synthesis of MDCA and UDCA. Firstly, an improved high-throughput detection method for UDCA was developed to accelerate the screening process for mutants with improved UDCA synthesis capabilities. Next, the mutant S240A w
papers.summary: Researchers engineered the P450 OleP enzyme to more efficiently convert lithocholic acid into therapeutically useful bile acids (MDCA and UDCA). They developed a high-throughput screening method and identified the S240A mutant, which significantly improved catalytic activity for potential large-scale production.

papers.title: 3D-QSAR Modeling of the Anti-Inflammatory Activity of 28 Rare Biflavones.
papers.abstract: Inflammation underlies a wide range of chronic diseases. Current treatments, such as nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, are effective, but they often result in adverse side effects. This has driven the search for alternative anti-inflammatory agents with improved safety profiles. Biflavones have emerged as promising candidates, yet existing studies primarily focus on naturally occurring biflavones that are limited to those with C3'-C8″ linkages. In this study, we explore the anti-inflammatory potential of 28 biflavones featuring the underexplored C2'-C6″ linkage. Using LPS-induced RAW 264.7
papers.summary: This study investigates the anti-inflammatory potential of 28 rare biflavones with an underexplored C2'-C6″ linkage, using 3D-QSAR modeling. The findings identify key structural features that contribute to anti-inflammatory activity, offering a foundation for designing safer alternatives to current anti-inflammatory drugs.

papers.title: Design, Synthesis and Biological Effects Studies of Novel EGFR Inhibitors Targeting Wild-Type and Mutant EGFR (EGFR-L858R and EGFR-L858R/T790M).
papers.abstract: Lung cancer remains one of the most significant global health challenges. Although EGFR inhibitors are actively employed in treatment, there is an urgent need for novel and effective inhibitors. In this context, a series of new EGFR inhibitors targeting both wild-type and mutant EGFR were designed and synthesized. The anticancer potentials of the synthesized derivatives were evaluated on A549 (lung cancer) and NIH/3T3 (healthy fibroblast) cell lines using the MTT method. Biological activity results revealed that the derivatives with 3,4-dichloro (2i) and 2,4-dichloro (2j) substitutions on the
papers.summary: Researchers designed and synthesized new EGFR inhibitors that target both wild-type and mutant forms of the receptor, aiming to improve lung cancer treatment. The compounds were tested on lung cancer cells and healthy cells, with two specific derivatives showing promising anticancer activity.

papers.title: Superionic lithium mobility in low symmetry Li7Si2S7I polymorph accessed via Si2S7 dimer reorientation.
papers.abstract: We report the experimental discovery of Li7Si2S7I0.89Cl0.11, a triclinic (P1̄) analogue of the recently discovered monoclinic (P21/n) Li7Si2S7I (LSSI), which retains the high computationally predicted Li+ ion conductivity of LSSI. Li7Si2S7I0.89Cl0.11, which is effectively a polymorph of LSSI, maintains the same ordered anion packing based on the packing of spheres in the NiZr intermetallic, however demonstrates a distinct ordering of the Si4+ framework-forming cations. While both structures feature Si2S7 dimers within hexagonal close-packed (hcp) anion motifs, their different arrangement in th
papers.summary: This study reports the experimental discovery of a new polymorph of a lithium-ion conductor, Li7Si2S7I0.89Cl0.11, which retains high lithium mobility despite having a different arrangement of its structural framework. The finding shows that the ordering of silicon-based dimers can be altered without sacrificing ionic conductivity, offering new design principles for solid-state battery materials.

papers.title: Surface-specific film assembly of a Vibrio cholerae adhesin-derived peptide modulated by environmental salts.
papers.abstract: Underwater adhesion research increasingly draws on bioinspired systems to uncover the molecular mechanisms that enable strong interfacial binding in aqueous environments. The biofilm adhesin Bap1 from Vibrio cholerae contains a short peptide motif, SYWFFGWHTK (CP), which exhibits exceptional adhesive performance, surpassing mussel foot protein mfp5 under comparable conditions. Despite its promise, the roles of ionic environments and aggregation behavior in governing CP adhesion remain unclear. In this study, we investigate how ion identity influences CP aggregation, film formation, and interfa
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study investigates how different environmental salts influence the aggregation and film formation of a short peptide adhesin from Vibrio cholerae, which shows strong underwater adhesion. The findings reveal that specific ions modulate the peptide's surface assembly, providing insights into bioinspired adhesive design.

papers.title: Ultrafast excited-state proton transfer dynamics using linearized pair-density functional theory.
papers.abstract: Accurate simulation of excited-state bond-rearrangement dynamics remains a major challenge since photoinduced reactions can often involve significant changes in electronic structure along excited-state reaction pathways. Describing such processes requires an electronic structure method that provides balanced descriptions of all electronic states across the nuclear configuration space, while remaining computationally feasible for molecular dynamics. Linearized pair-density functional theory (L-PDFT) provides an efficient multireference framework for excited-state simulations by enabling an accu
papers.summary: This paper presents a new computational method, linearized pair-density functional theory (L-PDFT), that efficiently and accurately simulates excited-state proton transfer dynamics, a key process in photochemistry. The method balances accuracy and computational cost, making it feasible for molecular dynamics simulations of bond-rearrangement reactions.

papers.title: Simulation of cell-size systems at long timescales with flexible protein structures.
papers.abstract: Protein behavior inside cells is dominated by the crowded nature of the intracellular environment. Progress in structure determination of proteins and protein complexes, based on advances in Artificial Intelligence, provides an opportunity for structure-based modeling of cellular phenomena. Such modeling at the atomic resolution has been advanced by the traditional simulation techniques, e.g. molecular dynamics. A recently developed docking-based approach implements Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling of intermolecular energy landscapes, offering several orders of magnitude faster simulation pro
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This paper presents a new computational method that uses Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling to simulate protein interactions in crowded cellular environments much faster than traditional molecular dynamics. The approach leverages AI-based protein structure predictions to model flexible proteins over long timescales, enabling structure-based simulations of whole cells that were previously computationally infeasible.

papers.title: HCGT-PL: a heterogeneous contrastive graph transformer unifying protein-ligand affinity prediction and structure-based virtual screening.
papers.abstract: Structure-based virtual screening and binding affinity prediction remain challenging due to solvation/entropy effects, protein flexibility, and induced fit. We present a Heterogeneous Contrastive Graph Transformer for Protein-Ligand (HCGT-PL) framework. Each complex is represented as a directed heterogeneous graph with multiple node and relation types; relation-specific multi-head attention enables message passing and aggregation. Unsupervised augmentations yield transferable interaction representations that are fine-tuned for affinity regression and virtual screening. Across diverse benchmark
papers.summary: This paper introduces HCGT-PL, a new deep learning framework that represents protein-ligand complexes as directed heterogeneous graphs to improve both binding affinity prediction and virtual screening. The model uses relation-specific attention and unsupervised contrastive learning to capture transferable interaction patterns, outperforming existing methods on diverse benchmarks.

papers.title: In silico design of novel CTL based multi epitope vaccine for esophageal cancer using immunoinformatics and molecular docking.
papers.abstract: Esophageal cancer is an aggressive malignancy with high morbidity, mortality, and limited durable treatment options due to tumor heterogeneity, immune evasion, and recurrence. This study addresses these challenges by computationally designing a novel CTL-based multi-epitope vaccine using experimentally validated epitopes from cancer-testis antigens (NY-ESO-1 and MAGE-A family), which are overexpressed in esophageal squamous cell carcinoma. To the best of our knowledge, this represents one of the most comprehensive in silico investigations for esophageal cancer, uniquely integrating experimenta
papers.summary: This study computationally designed a multi-epitope vaccine for esophageal cancer by selecting experimentally validated epitopes from cancer-testis antigens overexpressed in esophageal squamous cell carcinoma. Using immunoinformatics and molecular docking, the authors constructed a vaccine construct and evaluated its stability and immune response potential, representing a comprehensive in silico investigation for this cancer type.

papers.title: Computational evaluation of the interactions of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons with a human receptor via DFT and molecular docking.
papers.abstract: Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs) are produced whenever there is burning from energy sources, such as coal, oil, gas, wood, garbage, and tobacco. When these chemicals are produced, they tend to bind to or form small particles in the air, water, and soil. While some Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons are naturally occurring, others are produced by industrial activities, causing potential toxicity to humans, and are reported to interfere with the function of cellular membranes and related enzymes. It has been reported that several carcinogenicity and mutagenicity effects can be traced to the
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods (DFT and molecular docking) to evaluate how polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) interact with a human receptor, providing insights into their potential toxicity mechanisms. The findings help explain how these environmental pollutants may interfere with cellular functions and contribute to carcinogenic effects.

papers.title: Identification of PDE10A binding ligands as potential therapeutics for the Parkinson's disease through a computational approach.
papers.abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disease characterized by the loss of dopaminergic neurons and resulting in physical and mental issues. Dopamine-based treatments have long been used for Parkinson's disease; however, they do not provide a complete cure, highlighting the need for alternative therapeutic strategies. Phosphodiesterase-10 A (PDE10A), a dual-substrate enzyme regulates dopamine signalling by hydrolyzing cAMP and cGMP. In PD, dopamine depletion disrupts cyclic nucleotide signalling, impairing motor function and overactived PDE10A further reduces cAMP/cGMP le
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to identify potential drug candidates that bind to PDE10A, an enzyme involved in dopamine signaling, as a new therapeutic approach for Parkinson's disease. The authors found several promising ligands that could modulate PDE10A activity, which may help restore normal cyclic nucleotide signaling disrupted in Parkinson's.

papers.title: The systemic anti-diabetic effect of polyherbal formulation Varanadi Kashayam is mediated through GLP-1 secretion and DPP4 inhibition.
papers.abstract: Frontiers of health science increasingly emphasize systems and network medicine approaches for managing complex lifestyle diseases such as diabetes. In this context, systemic targets like incretin hormones and their modulators, particularly DPP4 inhibitors have gained prominence. Ayurveda, the Indian System of Medicine, with its clinically validated multicomponent formulations, offers a valuable resource for integrative therapeutic strategies with systemic mode of action.
papers.journal: Journal of Ayurveda and integrative medicine
papers.summary: This study investigates how the Ayurvedic polyherbal formulation Varanadi Kashayam exerts anti-diabetic effects by stimulating GLP-1 secretion and inhibiting DPP4, highlighting its potential as a systemic therapeutic for diabetes management. The findings support the integration of traditional multicomponent formulations into modern systems medicine approaches for complex diseases.

papers.title: The SepN-FraD structural module serves as an anchor and assembly platform for septal junctions in cyanobacteria.
papers.abstract: Intercellular communication is essential for multicellularity. In filamentous cyanobacteria such as Nostoc sp. PCC 7120, communication occurs through septal junctions (SJs) that traverse the septum and connect neighboring cells. Although the SJ components FraD and SepN are essential for SJ formation, their precise roles and spatial organization remain unclear. Here, we combine cryo-electron tomography, AlphaFold 3 structure prediction, and molecular dynamics simulation to refine the SJ architecture. We identify previously unresolved structural features, including a continuous, membrane-like tu
papers.summary: This study reveals how the SepN-FraD protein module acts as an anchor and assembly platform for septal junctions (SJs) in filamentous cyanobacteria, which are essential for intercellular communication. Using cryo-electron tomography, AlphaFold 3, and molecular dynamics, the authors identify new structural features of SJs, including a continuous membrane-like structure, advancing our understanding of multicellularity in these bacteria.

papers.title: Integrative immunoinformatics and structural modeling for the rational design of a multi-epitope vaccine candidate against human cytomegalovirus.
papers.abstract: Human cytomegalovirus (CMV) is a globally widespread pathogen associated with significant morbidity in immunocompromised individuals. Despite its clinical importance, no licensed vaccine is currently available. This study aimed to design a rational multi-epitope vaccine candidate targeting CMV using an integrative approach combining immunoinformatics and structural biology. Viral proteins were screened to identify epitopes with high affinity for B cells, cytotoxic T cells (CTLs), and helper T cells (HTLs) using the Immune Epitope Database (IEDB). Selected epitopes were filtered according to th
papers.summary: This study used computational methods to design a multi-epitope vaccine candidate against human cytomegalovirus (CMV), for which no licensed vaccine currently exists. By screening viral proteins for B cell and T cell epitopes and modeling the resulting vaccine structure, the authors propose a rationally designed candidate that could potentially be tested experimentally.

papers.title: Decoding cytokine interactions for psoriasis therapy through computational repurposing of antihypertensive drugs.
papers.abstract: Psoriasis is a chronic, immune-mediated skin disorder characterized by excessive inflammation and the overexpression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, including IL-6, IL-1β, IL-17 A, and IFN-γ. Despite the availability of targeted therapies, current treatments often entail high costs, limited accessibility, and adverse side effects. Drug repurposing-utilizing approved medications for novel indications-offers a cost-effective strategy to accelerate the development of alternative therapies with established safety profiles. Twelve FDA-approved antihypertensive drugs-including ACE inhibitors, angiote
papers.summary: This study computationally screens FDA-approved antihypertensive drugs for their potential to treat psoriasis by targeting key inflammatory cytokines. The authors identify several candidates that may modulate IL-6, IL-17A, and other psoriasis-related cytokines, suggesting a cost-effective repurposing strategy for this chronic skin condition.

papers.title: Discovery of indolylchalcone benzenesulfonamides as selective inhibitors of tumor-associated carbonic anhydrase IX and XII.
papers.abstract: Indole, characterized by its favorable bioavailability, unique structural attributes, and a broad pharmacological profile, represents a privileged scaffold in anticancer drug discovery. Chalcones constitute another important scaffold in medicinal chemistry and numerous chalcone derivatives have been reported to exhibit diverse biological activities, including anticancer and enzyme inhibitory effects. Several chalcone-based compounds have also been investigated as carbonic anhydrase inhibitors. Sulfonamides are a classical class of carbonic anhydrase (CA, EC 4.2.1.1) inhibitors with diverse the
papers.summary: This study reports the design and synthesis of novel indolylchalcone benzenesulfonamide compounds that selectively inhibit tumor-associated carbonic anhydrase isoforms IX and XII, which are important targets in cancer therapy. The compounds combine three privileged scaffolds—indole, chalcone, and sulfonamide—to achieve selective inhibition over off-target CA isoforms. This work provides a promising starting point for developing targeted anticancer agents with reduced side effects.

papers.title: Molecular dynamics investigation of interfacial energy and mechanical behavior in braid-reinforced hollow fiber membranes.
papers.abstract: This study presents a molecular dynamics investigation of braid-reinforced hollow fiber membranes to elucidate the interfacial and mechanical behaviors of polymeric composites composed of cellulose acetate (CA) and polyacrylonitrile (PAN). The analysis focuses on three representative configurations, homogeneous (CA/CA-II), semi-heterogeneous (PAN|CA/CA-I), and heterogeneous hybrid (CA/PAN-III), to evaluate their interfacial energies, adhesion mechanisms, and tensile responses. The calculated interfacial energies of - 6.32077, - 5.69262, and - 4.71113 mJ/m2 for CA/CA-II, PAN|CA/CA-I, and CA/PAN
papers.summary: This study uses molecular dynamics simulations to analyze the interfacial energy and mechanical behavior of braid-reinforced hollow fiber membranes made from cellulose acetate and polyacrylonitrile. The authors evaluate three polymer configurations and find that homogeneous interfaces have the strongest adhesion, while heterogeneous interfaces show weaker bonding but distinct mechanical properties.

papers.title: Anti-inflammatory Potential of Apigenin and Luteolin in Oral Mucositis: An In Silico Docking Study Targeting IL-6, NF-κB, and COX-2.
papers.abstract: Oral mucositis (OM) is an oral mucosal inflammation induced by radiotherapy/chemotherapy, which is characterized by erythema, edema, and oral ulceration that significantly reduce patients' quality of life. Several pro-inflammatory cytokines such as interleukin-6 (IL-6), nuclear factor kappa B (NF-κB), and cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) are involved in the pathophysiological stage of OM. Natural flavonoids, for instance, apigenin and luteolin, have demonstrated anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. This study aimed to evaluate the anti-inflammatory potential of apigenin and luteolin through a
papers.summary: This study used computer modeling to test whether two natural compounds found in plants, apigenin and luteolin, could bind to and potentially block three key inflammatory proteins (IL-6, NF-κB, and COX-2) involved in oral mucositis, a painful side effect of cancer treatment. The in silico docking analysis suggests both flavonoids have promising anti-inflammatory potential against these targets.

papers.title: Lenacapavir-induced lattice hyperstabilization is central to HIV-1 capsid failure at the nuclear pore complex and in the cytoplasm.
papers.abstract: Lenacapavir (LEN) is the first human immunodeficiency virus type 1 (HIV-1) capsid inhibitor approved for clinical use in humans. It inhibits multiple steps of the viral life cycle; however, the molecular details of the effect of LEN on capsid structure and the mechanistic steps of the inhibition are not understood. Recent studies show that intact cone-shaped capsids and capsids with LEN-induced breaks can dock at nuclear pore complexes (NPCs), but only intact capsids enter the nucleus. In this work, we combined large-scale coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations and live-cell imaging to
papers.summary: This study reveals that the HIV-1 drug lenacapavir works by hyperstabilizing the viral capsid lattice, which prevents the capsid from properly interacting with nuclear pore complexes and blocks nuclear entry. The combination of molecular dynamics simulations and live-cell imaging shows that while drug-induced broken capsids can still dock at the nuclear pore, only intact capsids can enter the nucleus. This provides a mechanistic understanding of how lenacapavir inhibits HIV-1 infection at multiple steps.

papers.title: Theoretical study on N(sp2)+-C(sp2)-H···O hydrogen bonds in the context of drug discovery.
papers.abstract: C-H···O hydrogen bonds are regarded as weak and overlooked in structure-based drug design. Here, we demonstrate through quantum-chemical calculations that N(sp2)⁺-C(sp2)-H···O hydrogen bonds involving pyridinium ions exhibit strong and directional interactions with carbonyl oxygen atoms. Density-functional-theory (DFT) and natural-bond-orbital (NBO) analyses indicated that cationization increases the positive charge on adjacent sp2 C-H protons, thereby enhancing their proton-donating ability. Fragment-based symmetry-adapted-perturbation-theory (F-SAPT) analyses revealed that these interactions
papers.summary: This study shows that N(sp2)+-C(sp2)-H···O hydrogen bonds involving pyridinium ions are stronger and more directional than typical C-H···O hydrogen bonds, as demonstrated by quantum-chemical calculations. The findings suggest these interactions could be useful in structure-based drug design, where they have been previously overlooked.

papers.title: A structure-based epitope tagging approach identifies vulnerable sites on the malarial P36-P52 protein complex for antibody-mediated neutralization of Plasmodium sporozoites.
papers.abstract: Malaria is caused by apicomplexan parasites of the genus Plasmodium, which are transmitted through the bite of Anopheles mosquitoes that inject sporozoites (SPZs) into the skin. SPZs migrate to and infect the liver for an initial round of replication. SPZs and liver stages have long been considered as ideal targets for malaria vaccines. The main SPZ surface protein, the circumsporozoite protein (CSP), is the target of currently approved malaria vaccines and prophylactic antibody therapies. Studies in rodent malaria models have shown that anti-CSP antibodies exert their protective effect mainly
papers.summary: This study uses a structure-based epitope tagging approach to identify vulnerable sites on the P36-P52 protein complex of Plasmodium sporozoites that can be targeted by antibodies for neutralization. The findings highlight potential new targets for malaria vaccine development beyond the circumsporozoite protein (CSP).

papers.title: Structure of the Beat-Side complex reveals their interaction is required for neuromuscular system wiring.
papers.abstract: Nervous system assembly is guided by the actions of cell surface receptors. In Drosophila, members of the beaten path (Beat) and sidestep (Side) protein families have been described as axon guidance receptor-cue pairs, in addition to roles in specifying synaptic connectivity in the optic lobe. To understand the molecular basis and specificity of Beat-Side interactions, we report here the structure of a Beat-Side complex, Beat-Vc bound to Side-VI. The structure shows a binding topology similar to other immunoglobulin superfamily receptors, especially PD-1-PD-L1, nectins, SynCAMs, Dprs, and DIPs
papers.summary: This study reports the first structure of a Beat-Side protein complex (Beat-Vc bound to Side-VI), revealing a binding topology similar to other immunoglobulin superfamily receptors like PD-1-PD-L1. The findings demonstrate that these interactions are essential for proper neuromuscular system wiring in Drosophila, providing molecular insight into axon guidance and synaptic connectivity.

papers.title: Design and computational evaluation of E-stilbene-bearing 1,3,4-oxadiazole derivatives as potential tyrosinase inhibitors using DFT, molecular docking and ADMET studies.
papers.abstract: A series of E-stilbene-bearing 1,3,4-oxadiazole derivatives (4a-b and 5a-f) was synthesized via the Mizoroki-Heck reaction and evaluated as inhibitors of tyrosinase, a vital enzyme involved in melanin biosynthesis. The synthesized compounds were structurally confirmed through NMR spectroscopy (1H NMR, 13C NMR), infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and mass spectrometry. Nearly all derivatives exhibited inhibitory effects against tyrosinase with IC50 values ranging from 0.32 ± 0.03 µM to 6.28 ± 0.05 µM. Among them, compounds 5d and 5b illustrated the most significant inhibitory activity, with IC50 va
papers.summary: This study synthesized and tested new E-stilbene-based compounds as potential inhibitors of tyrosinase, an enzyme key to melanin production. Several compounds showed strong inhibitory activity, with the most potent having IC50 values as low as 0.32 µM, suggesting they could be developed for skin-lightening or anti-browning applications.

papers.title: ANGPTL4 contributes to osimertinib resistance through NDRG1-associated extracellular matrix remodeling in EGFR-mutant NSCLC organoid models.
papers.abstract: As a first-line treatment for EGFR-mutated non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), acquired resistance to osimertinib has become a major clinical challenge. Traditional two-dimensional cell culture models have significant limitations in simulating the tumor microenvironment, making it difficult to fully elucidate complex resistance mechanisms. In recent years, three-dimensional organoid models have emerged as a crucial platform for studying resistance mechanisms due to their superior ability to preserve tumor tissue architecture and heterogeneity. Angiopoietin-like protein 4 (ANGPTL4), a secreted
papers.summary: This study uses 3D organoid models of EGFR-mutant lung cancer to show that the protein ANGPTL4 drives resistance to the drug osimertinib by remodeling the extracellular matrix via NDRG1. The findings highlight a new mechanism of drug resistance that could be targeted to overcome treatment failure in patients.

papers.title: A comprehensive dataset of 32 million pentapeptide structures for high-throughput virtual screening.
papers.abstract: Small peptides are widely used as binders, modulators, and structural motifs, but their conformational flexibility complicates structure-based analysis and high-throughput screening. We present an open dataset of three-dimensional structures for the complete space of canonical amino-acid pentapeptides: 3,200,000 unique sequences with up to 10 conformers per sequence, for a total of 32,000,000 peptide conformers. Structures were generated directly from sequence using an automated workflow built on UCSF ChimeraX for model construction, Reduce for hydrogen placement, and RDKit for conformer gener
papers.summary: Researchers generated an open dataset of 32 million 3D structures covering all possible pentapeptide sequences (3.2 million unique sequences, each with up to 10 conformers). This resource enables high-throughput virtual screening of small peptides for drug discovery and structural biology without requiring experimental structure determination.

papers.title: ScrambleBench: a workflow for comparative assessment of structure-based de novo generative models.
papers.abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (AI) has rapidly advanced over the past decade in the field of drug discovery, particularly for the de novo design of small molecules based on target protein structures. While generative AI has the potential to complement traditional structure-based drug design (SBDD) to discover novel hits, their performance is often assessed using non-standardized evaluation criteria. As the number of generative AI models continue to grow, it becomes increasingly important to determine whether these tools are sufficiently robust and reliable for integration into medicinal c
papers.summary: This paper introduces ScrambleBench, a standardized workflow for comparing structure-based de novo generative AI models used in drug discovery. The authors address the lack of consistent evaluation criteria by providing a benchmark to assess the robustness and reliability of these models for medicinal chemistry applications.

papers.title: Metastable multimeric G-quadruplex 2'FY-RNA aptamers that selectively bind pyoverdines.
papers.abstract: Two 2'FY-RNA aptamers with distinct sequences were selected for specific binding to pyoverdine-Pf5 (PVD-Pf5), increasing chromophore fluorescence upon binding. They also recognized the peptide portion of pyoverdines, as shown by their differential specificity for related variants. Computational analysis and experimental data (NMM binding, CD spectra) identified G-quadruplex structures that were thermally metastable but reformed in the presence of PVD-Pf5. Further structural studies mainly with one aptamer revealed imino proton peaks in 1D H-NMR and pressure stability up to 2 kbar. Electrophore
papers.summary: Researchers developed RNA aptamers that bind specifically to a bacterial signaling molecule (pyoverdine) and increase its fluorescence. The aptamers form metastable G-quadruplex structures that are stabilized by the target, enabling selective recognition of different pyoverdine variants. This work provides a new tool for detecting and studying bacterial siderophores.

papers.title: Structural and biochemical insights into the antibiofilm activity of cationic lipoamino acid ester against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus.
papers.abstract: Three-dimensional structures, primarily derived from X-ray crystallography, are crucial for understanding the solid-state properties of active pharmaceuticals and for enabling structure-based drug design. Herein, we report the first single crystal X-ray structure of cationic lipoaminoacid ester [(2-((N-(2-methoxy-2-oxoethyl)tetradecanamido)methyl)-1-methylpyridin-1-ium iodide, QPyN14Ge]. QPyN14Ge crystallized in the triclinic space group P1̄. QPyN14Ge has a highly ordered and coherent packing arrangement due to tail-to-tail interdigitation of hydrophobic layers, stabilization by planar trans-a
papers.summary: This study reports the first single-crystal X-ray structure of a cationic lipoamino acid ester (QPyN14Ge) and shows it has potent antibiofilm activity against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). The compound's ordered packing and ability to disrupt bacterial membranes explain its effectiveness against both planktonic and biofilm forms of MRSA.

papers.title: Engineering intrinsically radiolabeled nanoparticles for imaging and therapy: a decade of design strategies and insights.
papers.abstract: The convergence of nanotechnology and nuclear medicine has opened new possibilities for cancer imaging and therapy, but conventional chelator-based radiolabeling approaches often suffer from limited stability and can alter the inherent properties of nanomaterials. Intrinsic radiolabeling has emerged as a promising alternative, enabling the direct incorporation of radionuclides within the nanoparticle matrix through mechanisms such as lattice doping, isotopic substitution, coordination to structural sites, or entrapment during nanoparticle formation. In this approach, the radionuclide becomes a
papers.summary: This review summarizes a decade of design strategies for intrinsically radiolabeled nanoparticles, which incorporate radionuclides directly into the nanoparticle matrix to improve stability and preserve nanomaterial properties for cancer imaging and therapy. The authors discuss mechanisms like lattice doping and isotopic substitution, highlighting how this approach overcomes limitations of conventional chelator-based methods.

papers.title: N-MIX: an in silico framework for predicting ADAM10-mediated substrate cleavage sites through structural and spatial analysis of protease-substrate interactions.
papers.abstract: ADAM10 is a membrane-anchored metalloprotease that mediates ectodomain shedding of diverse substrates implicated in cancer, inflammation, and neurodegeneration. Despite its biological significance, ADAM10 substrate specificity remains poorly understood, as conventional sequence-based analyses have proven insufficient to identify conserved cleavage motifs. Through three-dimensional structural analysis of ADAM10-substrate complexes predicted by AlphaFold2, we identified four key determinants governing ADAM10-mediated proteolytic cleavage: (1) conserved hydrogen bond interactions at the enzyme-su
papers.summary: This paper presents N-MIX, a computational framework that uses AlphaFold2-predicted 3D structures to identify how ADAM10, a key protease in cancer and neurodegeneration, recognizes and cleaves its substrates. By analyzing structural features rather than just protein sequences, the authors discovered four key determinants of cleavage specificity, offering a new approach for predicting protease substrates.

papers.title: MLAC: MicroED-assisted ligand structure analysis in complexes and its application to hERG-ligand complexes.
papers.abstract: Accurate determination of ligand structures in protein-ligand complexes is essential for elucidating molecular recognition mechanisms and advancing structure-based drug discovery. Cryogenic electron microscopy (cryo-EM) has emerged as a powerful technique for determining macromolecular structures; however, reliable identification of small-molecule ligands from cryo-EM maps remains challenging, particularly in the absence of accurate initial ligand models. Here, we present MLAC (MicroED-assisted Ligand structure Analysis in Complexes), an integrative framework that combines microcrystal electro
papers.summary: MLAC is a new method that combines microcrystal electron diffraction (MicroED) with cryo-EM to accurately determine the structures of small-molecule ligands bound to proteins, addressing a key challenge in structure-based drug design. The authors demonstrate its utility by applying it to hERG-ligand complexes, showing improved ligand identification over cryo-EM alone.

papers.title: Invited review: Manufacturing Whey Protein Colloidal Particles via Liquid Antisolvent Precipitation Method: Particle Formation Mechanism and Ingredient Functionality Aspects.
papers.abstract: The liquid antisolvent (LAS) precipitation, also referred to as desolvation, has emerged as a promising strategy to manufacturing whey protein particles (WPP) with tunable structures and functionalities. In contrast with conventional heat-induced protein aggregation, protein aggregation induced by LAS processing is driven by protein supersaturation generated by a rapid reduction in solvent quality upon mixing with an antisolvent under ambient conditions. This review consolidates current knowledge on the mechanisms of LAS induced protein-protein aggregation, LAS processing variables, physicoche
papers.summary: This review discusses the liquid antisolvent (LAS) precipitation method for manufacturing whey protein particles, which offers tunable structures and functionalities compared to conventional heat-induced aggregation. The method relies on rapid solvent quality reduction to induce protein supersaturation and aggregation under ambient conditions.

papers.title: TEDLH: Domain HMMs for sensitive detection of remote homologues.
papers.abstract: The Encyclopedia of Domains (TED) provides domain annotations for proteins in the AlphaFold Protein Structure Database (AFDB) using a consensus of three state-of-the-art structure-based methods. We used these annotations to construct profile Hidden Markov models (HMMs), collectively forming the TED Library of HMMs (TEDLH). TEDLH enables sensitive sequence and profile searches, supporting systematic exploration of protein domain families and their evolutionary relationships.
papers.summary: The authors created a library of profile Hidden Markov models (TEDLH) based on domain annotations from the Encyclopedia of Domains (TED), which uses three structure-based methods to annotate proteins in the AlphaFold database. TEDLH enables sensitive detection of remote protein homologues through sequence and profile searches, facilitating the study of domain families and evolution.

papers.title: Atomistic Simulations of Fe(CO)5 Fragmentation Dynamics on a Substrate.
papers.abstract: The dissociation of organometallic molecules upon ionization by electron impact is one of the key fundamental processes behind nanofabrication using focused electron beams. The degree of molecular fragmentation (i.e., the number of volatile ligands produced) and the dynamics of this process are important factors affecting the elemental composition and metal content of the resulting deposits. This study presents results of irradiation-driven molecular dynamics (IDMD) simulations of the irradiation-induced fragmentation of iron pentacarbonyl, Fe-(CO)5, physisorbed on several different experiment
papers.journal: The journal of physical chemistry. C, Nanomaterials and interfaces
papers.summary: This study uses computational simulations to understand how iron pentacarbonyl molecules break apart when hit by electrons while sitting on a surface, which is important for nanofabrication techniques. The researchers found that the surface material influences how the molecule fragments, affecting the final metal content of the deposited material. This work provides insights that could help optimize electron-beam-based nanofabrication processes.

papers.title: Effect of Precompression on Detonation Performance and Products of Energetic Materials: Application to CL-20.
papers.abstract: The initial pressure plays an important role in the energy behavior of energetic materials (EMs) during combustion or detonation, but difficulties in experimental monitoring and the lack of equation-of-state data in numerical models make the effects of high initial pressure hard to estimate. To investigate the effects of preloading on the thermodynamic properties of CL-20, quantum mechanics (QM) calculations were performed under external pressures ranging from 1 atm to 50 GPa. As the pressure rises, the density increases from 2.04 g/cm3 to 3.38 g/cm3, and the enthalpy of formation increases to
papers.journal: The journal of physical chemistry. C, Nanomaterials and interfaces
papers.summary: This study uses quantum mechanics calculations to investigate how precompression affects the energetic material CL-20, finding that increasing pressure from 1 atm to 50 GPa raises density from 2.04 to 3.38 g/cm³ and increases enthalpy of formation. The research provides thermodynamic data that could help predict detonation performance under high initial pressures, addressing a gap in experimental monitoring and numerical modeling.

papers.title: Molecular modeling of 7-propanamide benzoxaboroles as CPSF3 inhibitors through docking-based 3D-QSAR and molecular dynamics simulations.
papers.abstract: Benzoxaboroles are boron-based heterocycles with broad therapeutic potential, including two FDA-approved drugs, tavaborole and crisaborole. It was reported that the discovery of 7-propanamide benzoxaboroles as novel potent anti-cancer agents, with compound 44 inhibiting cleavage and polyadenylation specific factor 3 (CPSF3), a key mRNA processing enzyme. By blocking CPSF3's RNA-binding function, compound 44 disrupts pre-mRNA cleavage and induces cancer cell death. In our current study, we systematically investigated the structure-activity relationships (SARs) of a series of 7-propanamide benzo
papers.summary: This study uses computational modeling to understand how 7-propanamide benzoxaboroles inhibit the mRNA processing enzyme CPSF3, a potential anti-cancer target. By combining docking, 3D-QSAR, and molecular dynamics simulations, the authors reveal key structural features driving activity, which could guide the design of improved CPSF3 inhibitors.

papers.title: Structure-based simulations of the full Flock House virus capsid reveal pathways and energetics of an infection-critical peptide externalization event.
papers.abstract: Viral capsids are metastable assemblies whose large-scale dynamics enable critical steps in infection, including the exposure of internal protein segments that interact with host membranes. Resolving the mechanisms underlying these transitions remains challenging because they involve rare, cooperative motions distributed across entire viral particles. Here, we develop a full-capsid structure-based molecular dynamics framework to investigate infection-relevant dynamics in the T=3 Flock House virus capsid, a model non-enveloped RNA virus. This native contact-centric coarse-grained representation
papers.summary: This study uses a full-capsid molecular dynamics simulation to reveal how the Flock House virus exposes a critical internal peptide needed for host infection. The simulations show that this externalization occurs through cooperative, large-scale motions of the capsid, providing a mechanistic pathway for this infection step. This work offers a new computational framework for studying the dynamics of viral particles that are difficult to observe experimentally.

papers.title: Integrating machine learning and structure-based simulations to prioritise Z4P as a mutation-resilient IRE1α inhibitor for breast cancer.
papers.abstract: Breast cancer is one of the most prevalent and lethal malignancies affecting women globally. The increasing resistance to current therapeutic strategies highlights the need for novel molecular targets. Inositol-requiring enzyme 1 alpha (IRE1α), a key sensor in the unfolded protein response (UPR), has emerged as a promising therapeutic target due to its role in tumour progression and survival. This study employed an integrative in silico approach combining machine learning, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulations to identify potent, non-toxic IRE1α inhibitors for breast cancer tre
papers.summary: This study used machine learning and molecular simulations to identify Z4P as a potential drug that inhibits the IRE1α protein, which is involved in breast cancer cell survival. The compound was predicted to be effective even against mutated forms of the protein, suggesting it could overcome drug resistance. The work demonstrates a computational pipeline for prioritizing cancer drug candidates.

papers.title: Computational and Experimental Development of a Novel Multi-Epitope Vaccine Candidate Against Bovine Leukaemia Virus.
papers.abstract: Bovine leukaemia virus (BLV) is the causative agent of enzootic bovine leukosis, a chronic infectious disease that causes significant economic losses in the dairy and beef industries worldwide. Despite extensive research, there is no licensed vaccine available for effective prevention and control of BLV infection.
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to design a multi-epitope vaccine candidate against Bovine Leukaemia Virus (BLV), which currently has no licensed vaccine. The approach combines bioinformatics predictions with experimental validation to identify promising epitopes that could stimulate an immune response. This work provides a foundation for developing an effective vaccine to control BLV-associated economic losses in cattle.

papers.title: Investigation and computational prediction of gating pore currents in NaV1.2 mutations across clinical phenotypes.
papers.abstract: Voltage sensor domains (VSDs) are integral and common regions within voltage-gated ion channels that play a crucial role in cellular signaling and excitability. Each VSD comprises four transmembrane segments (S1-S4) per domain. The transmembrane segment S4 contains positively charged amino acid residues at every third position, which serve as the channel's gating charges. Over the last decade, it has been shown that these gating charges are the target of many pathological mutations. This study investigates three gating-charge mutations in domain I of the NaV1.2 channel (R220G, R223I, and R223Q
papers.summary: This study investigates how specific mutations in the voltage sensor of the NaV1.2 ion channel cause gating pore currents, which are linked to various clinical conditions. Using computational predictions and experimental validation, the authors show that these mutations disrupt normal channel gating, leading to pathological electrical activity. The findings provide a mechanistic understanding of how these mutations contribute to disease phenotypes.

papers.title: Studying and Simulating the Impact of Plasma Activated Water (PAW) on Salmonella in Almond Products: A Molecular Dynamics Investigation.
papers.abstract: Salmonella remains one of the most prevalent foodborne pathogens worldwide, significantly contributing to gastrointestinal illnesses. Therefore, it is crucial to employ methods that can effectively reduce or eliminate the presence of this harmful pathogen in agricultural products. The current study employed a continuous production system of plasma-activated water (PAW) to control and eliminate Salmonella in almonds. The study examined the impact of key operational parameters, including water flow rate, gas mixture ratio (air:argon), and the duration of applying PAW. Results demonstrated that p
papers.summary: This study investigates the use of plasma-activated water (PAW) to eliminate Salmonella contamination on almonds, finding that specific operational parameters like water flow rate and gas mixture can effectively reduce pathogen levels. The research combines experimental approaches with molecular dynamics simulations to understand the underlying mechanisms. This work offers a potential non-thermal method for improving food safety in agricultural products.

papers.title: Targeting bromodomain and extraterminal proteins in cardiovascular disease: Pathological mechanisms and therapeutic applications.
papers.abstract: Bromodomain and extraterminal proteins have emerged as key epigenetic regulators that coordinate transcriptional programs fundamental to cardiovascular physiology and disease. Although bromodomain and extraterminal protein inhibitors exert multiple cardiovascular effects, they cannot fully explain clinical heterogeneity in outcomes and responses, highlighting the need to clarify the context- and disease-specific roles of individual bromodomain and extraterminal protein family members. This narrative review delineates the distinct molecular roles of bromodomain-containing protein 2, bromodomain
papers.journal: The Journal of international medical research
papers.summary: This review examines how bromodomain and extraterminal (BET) proteins, key epigenetic regulators, influence cardiovascular disease by coordinating gene transcription. It highlights that while BET inhibitors show cardiovascular effects, their clinical outcomes vary, underscoring the need to understand the specific roles of individual BET family members like BRD2 in different disease contexts.

papers.title: OrgNet+: towards robust protein stability prediction with convolutional neural networks.
papers.abstract: Predicting the effect of single-point mutations on protein stability is a central problem in molecular biology and protein engineering. Recent structure-based deep learning methods, particularly 3D convolutional neural networks (3D CNNs), have achieved strong predictive performance by leveraging high-resolution protein structures. However, proteins exist as heterogeneous conformational ensembles rather than single static structures, and the impact of conformational flexibility on structure-based ΔΔG predictors remains poorly characterized. Consequently, current models may yield unstable or eve
papers.summary: This paper introduces OrgNet+, a 3D convolutional neural network that improves protein stability prediction by accounting for conformational flexibility, addressing a key limitation of existing structure-based methods that rely on single static structures. The authors show that incorporating ensemble information leads to more robust predictions of mutation effects on protein stability.

papers.title: seq2ribo: structure-aware integration of machine learning and simulation to predict ribosome location profiles from RNA sequences.
papers.abstract: Ribosome dynamics are vital in the process of protein expression. Current methods rely on ribosome profiling (Ribo-seq), RNA-seq profiles, and full genomic context. This restricts their use in de novo sequence design, like messenger RNA (mRNA) vaccines. Simulation-only approaches like the Totally Asymmetric Simple Exclusion Process (TASEP) oversimplify translation by focusing solely on codon elongation times.
papers.summary: seq2ribo is a new computational method that combines machine learning with physical simulation to predict ribosome positioning on RNA sequences, using only the RNA sequence itself rather than requiring experimental data like Ribo-seq. This approach enables prediction for de novo sequences, such as those used in mRNA vaccine design, overcoming limitations of current methods that depend on genomic context or oversimplify translation dynamics.

papers.title: Structure-function relationship in FGF signaling: rational design of highly stable FGF2 variants and their effect on cell growth, metabolism, and survival.
papers.abstract: Fibroblast growth factor 2 (FGF2) is a canonical member of the fibroblast growth factor family with essential roles in embryonic development, tissue regeneration, and stem cell biology, where it supports pluripotency and high proliferative capacity. Consequently, FGF2 is widely used in experimental models of regeneration and stem cell maintenance; however, its rapid thermal degradation at physiological temperatures limits its practical utility and therapeutic potential. Despite the availability of stabilized variants, developing FGF2 analogs that combine exceptional thermodynamic resilience wi
papers.summary: This study describes the rational design of highly stable FGF2 variants by engineering specific mutations to improve thermal stability while preserving biological activity. The stabilized FGF2 variants effectively promote cell growth, metabolism, and survival, overcoming a key limitation of wild-type FGF2 for experimental and therapeutic applications.

papers.title: KASSPer: Kinase Active Site Structure Prediction using Protein and Ligand Language Models and Its Application to Virtual Screening.
papers.abstract: Structure-based virtual screening (SBVS) is limited by the rigid-receptor assumption, which is particularly problematic for kinases that adopt multiple active-site conformations but are experimentally biased toward a single state. Although ensemble screening can address this limitation, it remains computationally expensive.
papers.summary: KASSPer is a new computational method that predicts kinase active site structures using protein and ligand language models, enabling more accurate virtual screening without the computational cost of traditional ensemble approaches. It overcomes the limitation of rigid-receptor assumptions in structure-based drug discovery by modeling the conformational flexibility of kinase active sites.

papers.title: In Silico Drug Repositioning Identifies SYK Kinase Inhibitors as Potential Neuroprotective Agents for Ataxia-Telangiectasia.
papers.abstract: Ataxia-Telangiectasia (AT) is a rare neurodegenerative disorder characterized by progressive neuronal loss and chronic neuroinflammation. Emerging evidence indicates that aberrant overexpression of the adaptor protein TYROBP promotes sustained recruitment and activation of spleen tyrosine kinase (SYK), contributing to pathogenic inflammatory signaling in AT. In this study, we applied a drug repositioning strategy to identify clinically approved compounds that inhibit SYK activity. An integrated in silico workflow was employed, combining convolutional neural network (CNN)-based molecular dockin
papers.summary: This study uses a computational drug repositioning approach to identify FDA-approved SYK kinase inhibitors as potential treatments for Ataxia-Telangiectasia, a rare neurodegenerative disease. The authors developed an integrated in silico workflow combining CNN-based molecular docking to screen for compounds that could reduce neuroinflammation by targeting the TYROBP-SYK signaling pathway. The findings suggest that repurposing existing SYK inhibitors may offer a new therapeutic strategy for this disorder.

papers.title: Investigating the effect of progressive truncations at the ALS-linked protein TDP-43 RRM2 on its aggregation mechanism.
papers.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis is a neurodegenerative disease characterized by inclusions of TDP-43 protein. C-terminal fragments (CTFs) of TDP-43, generated by cleavage within its second RNA recognition motif (RRM2), have been found forming aggregates in patients. Aggregation has often been attributed to the C-terminal domain, but increasing evidence indicates that RRM2 fragments contribute to pathological inclusions. We performed extensive molecular dynamics simulations to investigate the changes resulting from the truncation that could lead to aggregation. We analyzed the full RRM2 domain (f
papers.summary: This study uses molecular dynamics simulations to investigate how progressive truncations in the RRM2 domain of the TDP-43 protein, which is linked to ALS, promote aggregation. The findings suggest that specific truncations within RRM2, not just the C-terminal domain, contribute to pathological protein inclusions. This work provides mechanistic insight into how cleavage of RRM2 may drive neurodegeneration.

papers.title: ELF3 promotes hyperglycemia-induced corneal epithelial senescence and IL-2 attenuates ELF3-associated signaling.
papers.abstract: Diabetic keratopathy constitutes a prevalent ocular complication of diabetes mellitus, clinically manifested as delayed corneal epithelial repair, recurrent epithelial defects, and potential corneal ulceration. Currently, the underlying molecular mechanisms and effective therapeutic strategies remain incompletely defined. This study integrates transcriptomic analysis with computational pharmacology and experimental validation. The results suggest that ELF3 is associated with corneal epithelial senescence in diabetes and identify IL-2 as a potential modulator of ELF3-associated signaling.
papers.summary: This study identifies the transcription factor ELF3 as a driver of corneal epithelial cell aging in diabetes, and shows that the immune signaling molecule IL-2 can counteract ELF3-related damage. The findings offer a new molecular target and potential therapeutic strategy for diabetic keratopathy, a common eye complication of diabetes.

papers.title: Convergent GenX biodegradation by genomically designed and functionally screened synthetic bacterial consortia.
papers.abstract: GenX (hexafluoropropylene oxide dimer acid) has been introduced as a replacement for legacy polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) compounds; however, accumulating evidence indicates this C6 ether-PFAS may exhibit comparable toxicity to PFOA while persisting in contaminated water supplies. Biological remediation offers genuine mineralization potential but has been constrained by the exceptional stability of carbon-fluorine bonds. Here, we demonstrate that synthetic bacterial consortia assembled through rational genomic design (GENIA) or microfluidic selection (Community G) achieve rapid GenX biode
papers.summary: Researchers developed synthetic bacterial consortia, either through genomic design or microfluidic selection, that can rapidly biodegrade GenX, a replacement PFAS compound with potential toxicity similar to PFOA. This work addresses the challenge of breaking stable carbon-fluorine bonds and offers a promising biological remediation approach for contaminated water supplies.

papers.title: Integrated machine learning and multi-omics analysis identifies CYP1B1 as a candidate target of orientin in intervertebral disc degeneration-associated low back pain.
papers.abstract: Orientin is a bioactive agent isolated from natural resources successfully applied for pain control. This study was to explore candidate hub gene of orientin to intervertebral disc degeneration (IDD) -associated low back pain.
papers.summary: This study uses machine learning and multi-omics data to identify CYP1B1 as a potential target of the natural compound orientin for treating low back pain associated with intervertebral disc degeneration. The findings suggest orientin may act through CYP1B1 to alleviate pain, offering a new candidate for drug development.

papers.title: Machine learning-driven discovery of antimicrobial peptides against Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
papers.abstract: Antibiotic resistance has become a global health crisis, driving the urgent need for novel antibacterial strategies. Antimicrobial peptides (AMPs) have emerged as promising therapeutic candidates due to their broad-spectrum activity and low propensity for resistance development. However, conventional discovery approaches remain time-consuming and inefficient for large-scale screening.
papers.summary: This study uses machine learning to rapidly identify antimicrobial peptides (AMPs) effective against the drug-resistant pathogen Pseudomonas aeruginosa, overcoming the limitations of traditional slow screening methods. The approach successfully discovered novel AMP candidates with potent antibacterial activity, offering a promising strategy to combat antibiotic resistance.

papers.title: Identification and validation of candidate biomarkers associated with mitochondria and 18 types of programmed cell death in trigeminal neuralgia: insights from transcriptome sequencing.
papers.abstract: Trigeminal neuralgia (TN) is relatively prevalent among the elderly population. Mitochondria-regulated programmed cell death has been identified as an important mechanism in the pathogenesis of a series of diseases; however, its role in TN remains poorly understood. This study aimed to uncover mitochondria-related genes (MRGs) and programmed cell death-related genes (PCDRGs) implicated in TN and elucidate their potential mechanisms.
papers.summary: This study identifies and validates candidate biomarkers linked to mitochondria and 18 types of programmed cell death in trigeminal neuralgia using transcriptome sequencing, revealing potential molecular mechanisms underlying the condition. The findings suggest that mitochondria-regulated cell death pathways may play a role in TN pathogenesis, offering new targets for diagnosis or therapy.

papers.title: Uncovering antioxidant and anti-breast cancer potential metabolite from streptomyces strain sp. VITAMB using integrated experimental and computational approaches.
papers.abstract: Actinobacteria's are rich in bioactive compounds, showing antimicrobial, antiviral, antioxidative, anti-inflammatory, and anticancer activity. The increasing demand for these metabolites in the culinary, pharmaceutical, cosmetic, and nutraceutical industries emphasizes the latent potential of actinomycetes. Paddy field ecosystem provides unique physicochemical conditions that favor the growth of actinomycetes, making them a valuable reservoir of pigment-producing microorganisms. In the present study, a yellow pigment-producing strain identified as Streptomyces sp (GenBank ID: OR958670) was iso
papers.summary: This study identifies a yellow pigment-producing Streptomyces strain from paddy soil with antioxidant and anti-breast cancer activity, using both lab experiments and computational modeling. The findings suggest this bacterial metabolite could be a promising candidate for developing natural antioxidant and anticancer therapies.

papers.title: Experimental and Mechanistic Validation of PARP1pred for Identifying Potent Leads.
papers.abstract: Poly(adenosine diphosphate-ribose) polymerase 1 (PARP1) is a pivotal target for treating homologous recombination-deficient cancers through the mechanism of synthetic lethality. While machine learning has accelerated the identification of novel inhibitors, many models lack experimental validation and high-resolution mechanistic insights. In this study, we evaluated the predictive robustness of the PARP1pred model using a hierarchical pipeline. Initial bioactivity predictions for candidates in unseen chemical space were validated through biochemical and cellular sensitivity assays using a panel
papers.summary: This study experimentally validates PARP1pred, a machine learning model for identifying PARP1 inhibitors, by testing its predictions in biochemical and cellular assays. The work demonstrates that computational predictions can be reliably confirmed through experimental validation, bridging the gap between in silico screening and practical drug discovery.

papers.title: Identification and experimental validation of CD74, PGLYRP1, and TXN as potential biomarkers in rheumatoid arthritis: an integrative bulk and ScRNA-seq study.
papers.abstract: Chronic joint inflammation, the hallmark of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), is an autoimmune condition that commonly leads to progressive joint damage and dysfunction. While several clinical biomarkers are available for diagnosing and predicting RA, their specificity and sensitivity are still insufficient. Therefore, the objective was to discover biomarkers associated with RA and delineate their functional mechanisms.
papers.summary: This study identified CD74, PGLYRP1, and TXN as potential biomarkers for rheumatoid arthritis by integrating bulk and single-cell RNA sequencing data, and experimentally validated their expression. These biomarkers may improve diagnostic specificity and sensitivity for RA, and the findings shed light on their functional roles in joint inflammation.

papers.title: Integrated multi-omics analysis reveals distinct microbiota-metabolite signatures and a novel HCN2-2-hydroxybutyric acid interaction in inflammatory bowel disease.
papers.abstract: Gut microbiota-derived short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) exert critical regulatory functions in inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). However, integrated profiling of fecal SCFA signatures alongside gut microbiota composition in ulcerative colitis (UC) and Crohn's disease (CD) remains insufficiently characterized. Furthermore, the molecular mechanisms through which microbiota metabolites engage host protein targets warrant systematic investigation.
papers.summary: This study profiles fecal short-chain fatty acids and gut microbiota in ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease, identifying distinct metabolite signatures for each subtype. It further uncovers a novel interaction between the host protein HCN2 and the metabolite 2-hydroxybutyric acid, suggesting a potential molecular mechanism linking microbiota metabolites to inflammatory bowel disease.

papers.title: Liposomal Nanoparticulate Drug Delivery Systems: Strategies to Destabilize Biological Membranes at the Target Tissue.
papers.abstract: Liposomal nanoparticulate drug delivery systems (LNDDSs) are clinically validated nanomedicine platforms seeing regular use in oncology and infectious disease. Their applications have rapidly expanded with several tissue targeting formulations in early-phase clinical trials. Beyond small molecular drugs, LNDDSs are increasingly employed for delivery of nucleic acid therapeutics, such as ribonucleic acid (RNA) based vaccines and immunomodulators. Recent advances in nanomaterials have enabled LNDDSs not only to transport therapeutic agents across systemic biological barriers but also to selectiv
papers.summary: This review discusses liposomal nanoparticulate drug delivery systems (LNDDSs), clinically validated platforms used in oncology and infectious disease, and their expanding applications in targeting tissues and delivering nucleic acid therapeutics like RNA vaccines. It highlights recent nanomaterial advances that enable LNDDSs to cross biological barriers and selectively destabilize target membranes for improved drug delivery.

papers.title: Design, Synthesis and Biological Evaluation of New Oxazolidinone Antimicrobial Agents with Low Monoamine Oxidase A Inhibition.
papers.abstract: Linezolid is an oxazolidinone antibacterial agent that acts by binding to the 23S rRNA of the bacterial 50S ribosomal subunit to inhibit protein synthesis and exhibits potent activity against Gram-positive pathogens and Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Although resistance to linezolid remains relatively low, its clinical use is limited by safety concerns arising from inhibition of monoamine oxidase A (MAO-A). To address this limitation, we designed and synthesized a series of novel oxazolidinone derivatives with reduced MAO-A inhibition and preserved antibacterial potency.
papers.summary: Researchers designed and synthesized new oxazolidinone antibiotics that retain antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria and Mycobacterium tuberculosis while reducing inhibition of monoamine oxidase A, a side effect that limits the clinical use of linezolid. The compounds show promise for safer treatment of resistant infections.
